lesson_title: std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.front: Can std::array hold class types (user-defined types)?
cards.back: Yes, std::array can hold any type including class types, as long as the type is copyable or movable.
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.text: When initializing a std::array of structs, you need {{c1::double braces}}: std::array<Point, 3> arr {{c2::{{ {1,2}, {3,4}, {5,6} }}}}
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.front: What is brace elision in the context of aggregate initialization?
cards.back: Brace elision allows you to omit inner braces when initializing nested aggregates, using single braces instead of double braces for convenience.
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.question: Is this code valid using brace elision?
cards.code:
struct Point { int x, y; };
std::array<Point, 2> arr { {1,2}, {3,4} };
cards.answer: Yes, this uses brace elision. The outer braces are for std::array, and inner braces initialize each Point.
cards.explanation: With brace elision, you can use single braces. The full form would be: std::array<Point, 2> arr {{ {1,2}, {3,4} }}
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.text: std::array is an {{c1::aggregate}} type, which means it has no user-declared constructors and uses {{c2::aggregate initialization}}
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.concept: When to use double braces vs brace elision for std::array
cards.explanation:
Double braces (no elision):
  • More explicit and clear
  • Recommended for complex nested types
  • Example: {{ {1,2}, {3,4} }}
Single braces (with elision):
  • More concise
  • Works in most cases
  • Example: { {1,2}, {3,4} }
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.front: What happens when you value-initialize a std::array of class types with {}?
cards.back: Each element is value-initialized. For class types with default constructors, the default constructor is called. For aggregates, all members are value-initialized.
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.text: Using CTAD with std::array of structs: {{c1::std::array}} arr { Point{1,2}, Point{3,4} }; (no template parameters needed)
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.question: What's wrong with this initialization?
cards.code:
struct Point { int x, y; };
std::array<Point, 3> arr { 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 };
cards.answer: Missing braces for each Point struct. Should be: { {1,2}, {3,4}, {5,6} }
cards.explanation: Each Point needs its own pair of braces to initialize its x and y members. The compiler can't automatically group the values.
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.front: How do you create a std::array of objects where each element is default-constructed?
cards.back: Use empty braces: std::array<MyClass, 5> arr{}; This value-initializes each element, calling the default constructor for each.
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision

cards.text: When std::array is a member of a class, it can be initialized using {{c1::member initializer list}} in the constructor
cards.source: 17.4 - std::array of class types, and brace elision
